reasoning: The historical base rate for pandemics is roughly 2-5% per year (with only a handful of pandemics declared in the last century, such as in 1918, 1957, 1968, 2009, and 2020). While recent concerns about H5N1 avian influenza spreading in mammal populations have slightly elevated the short-term risk, the baseline probability of a new pandemic reaching the threshold of a formal WHO declaration in any single calendar year remains very low. Without evidence of an ongoing human-to-human transmission event of a novel pathogen, defaulting to the historical base rate (around 5%) is the most well-calibrated approach.
